lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Mistress! what, mistress! Juliet! fast, I warrant her, she:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Why, lamb! why, lady! fie, you slug-a-bed!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Why, love, I say! madam! sweet-heart! why, bride!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: What, not a word? you take your pennyworths now;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Sleep for a week; for the next night, I warrant,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: The County Paris hath set up his rest,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: That you shall rest but little. God forgive me,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Marry, and amen, how sound is she asleep!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I must needs wake her. Madam, madam, madam!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ay, let the county take you in your bed;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: He’ll fright you up, i’ faith. Will it not be? Undraws the curtains.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: What, dress’d! and in your clothes! and down again!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I must needs wake you: Lady! lady! lady!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Alas, alas! Help, help! my lady’s dead!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O, well-a-day, that ever I was born!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Some aqua vitae, ho! My lord! my lady!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: What noise is here?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O lamentable day!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: What is the matter?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Look, look! O heavy day!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: O me, O me! My child, my only life,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Revive, look up, or I will die with thee!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Help, help! Call help.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: For shame, bring Juliet forth; her lord is come.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: She’s dead, deceased, she’s dead; alack the day!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Alack the day, she’s dead, she’s dead, she’s dead!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Ha! let me see her: out, alas! she’s cold;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Her blood is settled, and her joints are stiff;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Life and these lips have long been separated:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Death lies on her like an untimely frost

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Upon the sweetest flower of all the field.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O lamentable day!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: O woeful time!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Death, that hath ta’en her hence to make me wail,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Ties up my tongue, and will not let me speak.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Come, is the bride ready to go to church?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Ready to go, but never to return.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: O son! the night before thy wedding-day

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Hath Death lain with thy wife. There she lies,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Flower as she was, deflowered by him.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Death is my son-in-law, Death is my heir;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: My daughter he hath wedded: I will die,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And leave him all; life, living, all is Death’s.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Have I thought long to see this morning’s face,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: And doth it give me such a sight as this?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Accursed, unhappy, wretched, hateful day!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Most miserable hour that e’er time saw

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: In lasting labour of his pilgrimage!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: But one, poor one, one poor and loving child,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: But one thing to rejoice and solace in,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: And cruel death hath catch’d it from my sight!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O woe! O woeful, woeful, woeful day!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Most lamentable day, most woeful day,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: That ever, ever, I did yet behold!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O day! O day! O day! O hateful day!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Never was seen so black a day as this:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O woeful day, O woeful day!

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Beguiled, divorced, wronged, spited, slain!

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Most detestable death, by thee beguiled,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: By cruel cruel thee quite overthrown!

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: O love! O life! not life, but love in death!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Despised, distressed, hated, martyr’d, kill’d!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Uncomfortable time, why camest thou now

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: To murder, murder our solemnity?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: O child! O child! my soul, and not my child!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Dead art thou! Alack! my child is dead;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And with my child my joys are buried.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Peace, ho, for shame! confusion’s cure lives not

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In these confusions. Heaven and yourself

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Had part in this fair maid; now heaven hath all,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And all the better is it for the maid:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Your part in her you could not keep from death,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But heaven keeps his part in eternal life.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The most you sought was her promotion;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For ’twas your heaven she should be advanced:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And weep ye now, seeing she is advanced

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Above the clouds, as high as heaven itself?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: O, in this love, you love your child so ill,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: That you run mad, seeing that she is well:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: She’s not well married that lives married long;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But she’s best married that dies married young.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Dry up your tears, and stick your rosemary

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: On this fair corse; and, as the custom is,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In all her best array bear her to church:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For though fond nature bids us all lament,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Yet nature’s tears are reason’s merriment.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: All things that we ordained festival,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Turn from their office to black funeral;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Our instruments to melancholy bells,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Our wedding cheer to a sad burial feast,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Our solemn hymns to sullen dirges change,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Our bridal flowers serve for a buried corse,

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And all things change them to the contrary.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Sir, go you in; and, madam, go with him;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And go, Sir Paris; everyone prepare

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To follow this fair corse unto her grave:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The heavens do lour upon you for some ill;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Move them no more by crossing their high will.

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: Faith, we may put up our pipes, and be gone.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Honest good fellows, ah, put up, put up;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For, well you know, this is a pitiful case.

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: Ay, by my troth, the case may be amended.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Musicians, O, musicians, “Heart’s ease, Heart’s ease:” O, an you will have

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: me live, play “Heart’s ease.”

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: Why “Heart’s ease”?

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: O, musicians, because my heart itself plays “My heart is full of

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: woe:” O, play me some merry dump, to comfort me.

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: Not a dump we; ’tis no time to play now.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: You will not, then?

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: I will then give it you soundly.

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: What will you give us?

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: No money, on my faith, but the gleek; I will give you the minstrel.

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: Then I will give you the serving-creature.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Then will I lay the serving-creature’s dagger on your pate. I will

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: carry no crotchets: I’ll re you, I’ll fa you; do you note

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: me?

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: An you re us and fa us, you note us.

lines.speaker: SECOND MUSICIAN
lines.text: Pray you, put up your dagger, and put out your wit.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Then have at you with my wit! I will dry-beat you with

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: an iron wit, and put up my iron dagger. Answer me like

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: men: “When griping grief the heart doth wound, And doleful dumps the

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: mind oppress, Then music with her silver sound”— why “silver sound”? why

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: “music with her silver sound”? What say you, Simon Catling?

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: Marry, sir, because silver hath a sweet sound.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Pretty! What say you, Hugh Rebeck?

lines.speaker: SECOND MUSICIAN
lines.text: I say “silver sound,” because musicians sound for silver.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: Pretty too! What say you, James Soundpost?

lines.speaker: THIRD MUSICIAN
lines.text: Faith, I know not what to say.

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: O, I cry you mercy; you are the singer: I will say

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: for you. It is “music with her silver sound,” because musicians have

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: no gold for sounding: “Then music with her silver sound With speedy

lines.speaker: PETER
lines.text: help doth lend redress.”

lines.speaker: FIRST MUSICIAN
lines.text: What a pestilent knave is this same!

lines.speaker: SECOND MUSICIAN
lines.text: Hang him, Jack! Come, we’ll in here; tarry for the mourners, and stay dinner.

stage_directions.text: Enter Friar Laurence and Paris, with Musicians.
Exeunt Capulet, Lady Capulet, Paris, and Friar Laurence.

glosses.definition: sluggard; lazy sleeper — the Nurse's affectionate scold
dressed in her day clothes — but dead
the Nurse's diagnosis — death as heavenly theft

glosses.phrase: Death lies on her like an untimely frost
glosses.definition: Capulet's elegiac image — frost killing the fairest spring-flower
lamentable formula repeated many times — the mourners' ritual chorus
the wedding is now a funeral
put away — the musicians' instruments are no longer needed
sweet-toned — a common epithet for music

annotations.title: The mourners' ritual — comedy inside tragedy
annotations.body: The Nurse, Lady Capulet, Paris, and Capulet each deliver stylized laments over Juliet's 'corpse.' The repetitions — 'O lamentable day!', 'O woeful day!' — are so formulaic that they verge on parody. Shakespeare is doing something delicate here: the audience knows Juliet is not dead (she has drunk the Friar's potion), so the mourners' grief is both real and misdirected. The scene then pivots to the musicians and the servant Peter in a small comic interlude (Peter calling for the musicians to play Heart’s ease), giving the audience a pressure-release before Act 5. It is unusual theater — comedy embedded in a false funeral. Some editors consider the Peter scene to be a detachable bit for the comedian Will Kemp; others defend it as Shakespeare's deliberate tone-modulation before the tomb scene.
sources: Susan Snyder, The Comic Matrix of Shakespeare's Tragedies (1979)
Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: What tone does the scene of lamentation over Juliet's 'corpse' strike?
options: Formally stylized and repetitive, approaching parody — the audience knows Juliet is not really dead, so the mourners' grief is both real and misdirected

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare follow the funeral laments with a brief comic exchange with musicians?
options: The play is losing its thread
Deliberate tone-release before Act 5 — and a comic-register echo of Mercutio's lost voice; Peter the servant replaces Mercutio as wit-figure
To fill time while actors change costume
